text:
SURGICAL ONCOLOGY — PRE-OPERATIVE ASSESSMENT NOTE
Hepatobiliary and Pancreatic Surgery

PATIENT: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
ATTENDING: [Hepatobiliary Surgical Oncologist]
DICTATED BY: [Surgical Oncology Fellow]
VISIT TYPE: Pre-operative evaluation — pancreaticoduodenectomy

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old West African-descent woman with borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma who presents for pre-operative evaluation and clearance for planned pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure). She has completed approximately 8 months of multimodality neoadjuvant therapy, including 8 cycles of modified FOLFIRINOX followed by consolidative conventionally fractionated chemoradiation with concurrent cape (50.4 Gy in 28 fractions), and now presents for surgical reassessment and final pre-operative planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old retired schoolteacher with a background of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension who initially presented approximately 8 months ago with new-onset worsening of her diabetes and nonspecific epigastric discomfort. Workup at that time revealed a hypoenhancing mass in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas measuring 3.8 x 3.2 cm on multiphasic pancreatic protocol CT. EUS-guided fine needle biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (grade 2, infiltrating duct carcinoma). Staging workup including PET-CT confirmed locoregional disease with one FDG-avid peripancreatic lymph node and no evidence of distant metastases. CA 19-9 at diagnosis was markedly elevated at 1,247 U/mL. CEA was mildly elevated at 8.4 ng/mL. NGS on the tumor demonstrated KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, SMAD4 homozygous deletion, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and additional alterations; MSI-stable, TMB-low. Germline testing confirmed a heterozygous ATM pathogenic frameshift variant; BRCA2 variant was determined to be somatic-only on germline testing.

The case was reviewed at the multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board at the time of diagnosis. Given SMA abutment exceeding 180 degrees of circumference and direct SMV involvement with partial luminal narrowing, the tumor was deemed borderline resectable, and immediate surgery was not felt to offer an adequate chance of R0 resection. A neoadjuvant approach was recommended with the intent of converting the patient to resectable disease.

She proceeded with neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX: Eloxatin 85 mg/m2 (reduced to 68 mg/m2 after cycle 2 for grade 2 peripheral neuropathy), iri 150 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, and 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours (bolus 5-FU omitted). She completed 8 cycles in total. Cycle 6 required a 1-week dose delay due to grade 3 fatigue. Serial CA 19-9 monitoring demonstrated a robust decline: 1,247 → 620 (after cycle 2) → 310 (after cycle 4) → 85 U/mL (after cycle 8), representing a 93% reduction from baseline. Mid-treatment restaging CT after cycle 4 showed the primary tumor shrinking from 3.8 x 3.2 cm to 3.1 x 2.5 cm with improved SMA contact, decreased from >180 degrees to approximately 90-120 degrees. After all 8 cycles, the mass had further reduced to 2.6 x 2.1 cm with SMA contact at approximately 90 degrees or less.

Following completion of mFOLFIRINOX, the multidisciplinary tumor board recommended consolidative chemoradiation given residual borderline SMA contact. She received conventionally fractionated IMRT to 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction) with concurrent Xeloda 825 mg/m2 twice daily on radiation days, completed approximately 2 months ago. She tolerated chemoradiation with grade 2 nausea, grade 2 fatigue, grade 2 radiation gastritis (managed with escalated PPI), and grade 1 hand-foot syndrome from Xeloda. She lost approximately 3 kg during the chemoradiation course.

Restaging CT obtained approximately 4-6 weeks after completion of chemoradiation showed further reduction in the primary mass to 2.3 x 1.9 cm. SMA contact is now estimated at approximately 60-70 degrees of circumference. SMV appears patent with only minimal soft tissue thickening at the prior contact site. No new hepatic lesions, no adenopathy, no peritoneal disease. CA 19-9 at that time was 72 U/mL. The case was re-presented to the multidisciplinary tumor board, which confirmed resectability based on this favorable radiographic response and tumor marker trend. Surgical oncology was asked to evaluate for pancreaticoduodenectomy.

Ms. Osei-Bonsu reports that she has been recovering well from chemoradiation. Her epigastric discomfort has improved substantially since radiation. She notes mild persistent fatigue — much improved from the nadir during chemotherapy — which she rates as 3/10 in severity. She reports no new abdominal pain, no jaundice, no pruritus, no change in stool color or consistency (she has been monitoring given the prior biliary dilation). She has maintained her weight over the past 4-6 weeks after the losses during chemoradiation. Her appetite is fair. She is ambulatory without difficulty and is independent in all activities of daily living. She lives alone but has a daughter and son-in-law who will be available to provide post-operative support and who are aware of the planned surgery.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, head/uncinate process — as detailed above; status post neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX x8 cycles and consolidative chemoradiation (50.4 Gy with cape); now planning curative-intent Whipple procedure
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed approximately 10 years ago, worsening noted in the 3 months prior to cancer diagnosis (new-onset pancreatic diabetes component cannot be excluded); currently managed with metformin and has required insulin supplementation during treatment; most recent HbA1c 7.8% (improved from 8.9% at diagnosis)
3. Hypertension — well-controlled on amlodipine; no end-organ damage documented
4. Germline ATM heterozygous pathogenic frameshift variant (p.V2716fs) — confirmed on germline testing; family members counseled
5. Eloxatin-related peripheral sensory neuropathy — bilateral distal extremities, grade 1-2 at peak, now grade 1 and slowly improving
6. Radiation-induced gastritis — grade 2 during chemoradiation, resolving with PPI therapy

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY

- Appendectomy (remote, childhood, uncomplicated)
- No prior abdominal operations
- No prior hepatobiliary procedures

FAMILY HISTORY

- No known family history of pancreatic cancer
- Germline ATM heterozygous pathogenic variant identified in patient; family members (particularly first-degree relatives) have been advised to seek genetic counseling
- Mother: hypertension, deceased of cardiac disease in her 80s
- Father: type 2 diabetes mellitus, deceased of stroke in his 70s
- No known family history of breast, ovarian, or colorectal cancer

SOCIAL HISTORY

- Retired schoolteacher (over 30 years in primary school education)
- West African descent
- Never smoker
- Occasional alcohol use prior to diagnosis; abstinent since cancer diagnosis
- No illicit drug use
- Lives alone in a single-story home; daughter and son-in-law live nearby and are heavily involved in her care
- Excellent social support network including church community and extended family
- Has been referred to cancer survivorship services and social work for post-operative planning

ALLERGIES

- No known drug allergies
- No known contrast allergies (has received IV contrast for multiple CT scans without incident)

MEDICATIONS

1. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (diabetes management; will be held perioperatively)
2. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
3. Insulin glargine (Lantus) 18 units subcutaneous at bedtime (adjunctive glycemic control during treatment; dose titrated by endocrinology)
4. Insulin lispro (Humalog) sliding scale with meals as directed by endocrinology
5. Omeprazole 40 mg orally twice daily (radiation gastritis; dose escalated from 20 mg once daily)
6. Ondansetron 4 mg orally as needed for nausea (residual from chemoradiation course)
7. Loperamide 2 mg orally as needed for diarrhea (used intermittently)
8. Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg orally as needed for pain (mild)
9. Calcium carbonate + vitamin D supplement once daily (osteoporosis prevention; initiated given anticipated post-Whipple malabsorption)

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue mild (3/10), much improved from nadir during chemotherapy. Appetite fair to good. Weight loss approximately 3 kg during chemoradiation course; stable over past 4-6 weeks. No fever, no night sweats.
Gastrointestinal: Mild epigastric discomfort intermittently, improved markedly from prior. No jaundice, no pruritus. Bowel function largely normalized; soft-formed stools once to twice daily. No melena, no hematochezia, no hematoemesis. No significant nausea currently. No vomiting. Steatorrhea not present currently (pancreatic exocrine function presumably maintained preoperatively).
Hepatic/Biliary: No jaundice, no dark urine. Biliary dilation noted on original imaging has improved on subsequent scans. No biliary stenting was required.
Musculoskeletal/Neurologic: Mild bilateral distal hand and foot paresthesias (oxali-related peripheral neuropathy, grade 1, slowly improving). No functional limitation. No difficulty with fine motor tasks.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no dyspnea on exertion. Hypertension well-controlled.
Pulmonary: No cough, no hemoptysis, no dyspnea at rest or with moderate activity.
Endocrine: Glycemic control improved; checking blood sugars twice daily, values generally in target range per patient.
Skin: Grade 1 hand-foot changes (mild plantar erythema) essentially resolved after Xeloda discontinuation. Radiation field skin reaction fully healed.
Psychosocial: Patient reports anxiety around the upcoming surgery, which she acknowledges is expected. She is committed to proceeding and has discussed the risks and benefits extensively with her oncology team. Adequate coping and strong social support.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing West African-descent woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Appropriately anxious about upcoming surgery; appears well-nourished relative to prior visits. Wearing outpatient clothing in good repair.

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, heart rate 74 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16, temperature 36.7°C, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, weight 56.2 kg (BMI approximately 23.4 — down from 24.1 at baseline but stable over the past 4-6 weeks).

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. No conjunctival pallor. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular chains.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally in all four extremities. No lower extremity edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles. Respiratory effort unlabored.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. No voluntary or involuntary guarding. Mild deep epigastric tenderness to firm palpation in the right upper quadrant/epigastric region — present at baseline given prior radiation; no peritoneal signs. No palpable hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. No palpable abdominal mass appreciable on exam (tumor is deep and not palpable). No ascites clinically (no shifting dullness, no fluid wave). No caput medusae. The abdominal wall appears intact with no prior surgical scars other than a well-healed remote right lower quadrant appendectomy scar.

Musculoskeletal/Neurologic: Mild symmetric reduction in light touch and temperature sensation in bilateral distal feet to the ankle level and distal fingertips, consistent with grade 1 oxali-related peripheral sensory neuropathy. Motor strength intact in all four extremities (5/5). No ataxia on casual inspection. Deep tendon reflexes slightly diminished at the ankles bilaterally.

Skin: Well-healed radiation field (no visible active radiation dermatitis). Mild residual hyperpigmentation at the radiation field on the anterior abdomen. No active hand-foot skin reaction appreciated.

Lymph Nodes: No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count: WBC 5.8 x10^9/L, hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL (mild anemia, stable from prior), platelets 198 x10^9/L. No neutropenia.
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2), total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL (normal), direct bilirubin 0.2 mg/dL, AST 28 U/L, ALT 32 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 74 U/L, albumin 3.4 g/dL (mildly low, consistent with nutritional depletion from multimodality therapy), total protein 6.8 g/dL.
Coagulation: PT 12.8 seconds, INR 1.1 (normal). PTT 29 seconds.
HbA1c: 7.8% (improved from 8.9% at diagnosis; endocrinology co-managing; within acceptable perioperative range).
CA 19-9: 72 U/mL (markedly down from 1,247 U/mL at diagnosis; continues to trend down from 85 at end of mFOLFIRINOX). Note: this value is from the most recent restaging scan approximately 4-6 weeks ago; will recheck at pre-op blood draw.
CEA: 2.8 ng/mL (near-normal; down from 8.4 at diagnosis).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Most recent multiphasic CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (obtained approximately 4-6 weeks ago, 2 months after completing chemoradiation):

The pancreatic head mass has continued to shrink and now measures 2.3 x 1.9 cm — down from 3.8 x 3.2 cm at diagnosis. There has been progressive and consistent tumor reduction at every restaging evaluation throughout the neoadjuvant course. In terms of vascular involvement, the SMA relationship has improved substantially: the soft tissue contact is now estimated at approximately 60-70 degrees of circumference, a dramatic improvement from the initial >180-degree abutment. The SMV appears patent with only minimal residual soft tissue thickening at the prior contact site — no occlusion, no evidence of tumor encasement. No lymph nodes meeting criteria for malignant involvement are identified; the previously suspicious 1.2-cm peripancreatic node has decreased to 0.5 cm. No hepatic lesions are seen. No peritoneal disease. No pulmonary metastases on the chest component.

In my review of the imaging, I agree with the radiologic interpretation that this is a favorable response and that the anatomy is now consistent with technically resectable disease. The SMA relationship, while improved, still merits careful attention intraoperatively, and I have reviewed this with the operating team. I anticipate standard pancreaticoduodenectomy without planned vascular reconstruction, though the team is prepared for venous reconstruction if intraoperative findings dictate.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Impression: Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12V, TP53, SMAD4/CDKN2A deleted, MSS, germline ATM pathogenic variant) who has had an excellent response to a prolonged and well-tolerated neoadjuvant course. She has gone from a tumor that was felt to be unresectable at presentation — with SMA encasement exceeding 180 degrees — to one that now appears favorably positioned for curative-intent resection. This is a patient who has done everything right: tolerated 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX with dose modifications as expected, completed the full course of chemoradiation, and has demonstrated a sustained, progressive decline in CA 19-9 from 1,247 down to 72 U/mL. She is now ready for definitive surgical resection.

The multidisciplinary tumor board has reviewed the most recent restaging and has endorsed proceeding with surgery. I concur with this assessment. There is no evidence of distant metastatic disease. Performance status is ECOG 1. She is clinically well-nourished enough for surgery, though we will take extra precautions given mild pre-operative malnutrition (albumin 3.4).

1. Planned operation: Pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (PPPD/Whipple procedure) with curative intent. Classic versus pylorus-preserving technique was discussed with the patient; pylorus preservation is preferred given comparable oncologic outcomes and potential functional benefits. Standard regional lymphadenectomy will be performed. En bloc resection of the pancreatic head, uncinate process, duodenum, proximal jejunum (approximately 10-15 cm), gallbladder, distal common bile duct, and the second portion of the duodenum/ampullary region. Reconstruction via pancreaticojejunostomy (duct-to-mucosa preferred), hepaticojejunostomy, and duodenojejunostomy in standard fashion.

2. Vascular assessment: Based on current imaging, venous reconstruction of the SMV/portal vein is not anticipated to be necessary. However, the surgical team will have vascular surgery backup availability and the necessary tools for patch repair or segmental reconstruction should intraoperative findings suggest closer involvement than appreciated on imaging. SMA proximity of approximately 60-70 degrees should be separable with careful dissection; R0 intent at the SMA margin is critical, and frozen section of this margin will be sent intraoperatively.

3. Pre-operative optimization:
   - Nutritional support: Oral nutritional supplementation (Ensure or equivalent, two servings daily) recommended from now until day before surgery. Albumin 3.4 g/dL is mildly low but acceptable; a targeted 2-3 week pre-op nutritional window is appropriate and feasible within our surgical timeline. Patient counseled on high-protein diet.
   - Glycemic management: Endocrinology informed of surgical date. Current insulin regimen (Lantus 18 units at bedtime + lispro sliding scale) will be adjusted per our institutional perioperative diabetes protocol. Metformin held 48 hours pre-operatively and renal function rechecked prior. Target perioperative blood glucose 140-180 mg/dL. Patient and her daughter educated on monitoring and sick-day rules.
   - Cardiopulmonary clearance: Patient has no significant cardiac history. Functional capacity is adequate by history (can climb one flight of stairs without difficulty, walks 30 minutes daily). 12-lead ECG ordered; echocardiogram not felt to be necessary at baseline but will order if ECG is abnormal. Formal cardiology clearance not required per our institutional criteria; primary care physician notified.
   - Anesthesia pre-operative evaluation: Appointment scheduled. Will flag Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy, mild anemia, controlled diabetes, and history of radiation-induced gastritis (relevant for airway management and post-operative pain management strategy).
   - Bowel prep: Mechanical bowel prep with polyethylene glycol the day before surgery plus oral antibiotics (neomycin and erythromycin per our standard protocol).
   - Peri-operative antibiotics: Cefoxitin IV at induction; adjusted for allergy status (NKDA — no modification required).
   - DVT prophylaxis: Enoxaparin post-operatively (timing per anesthesia and surgeon agreement given planned epidural); pneumatic compression devices intraoperatively.
   - Medications: Continue amlodipine perioperatively. Hold metformin 48 hours pre-op. Insulin management as above. PPI (omeprazole) continued; will transition to IV pantoprazole perioperatively.

4. Informed consent: Extensive discussion held with Ms. Osei-Bonsu and her daughter today regarding the planned procedure. We reviewed the goals of surgery (curative intent, removal of the cancer with negative margins), the general risks of pancreaticoduodenectomy — including but not limited to: pancreatic leak/fistula (approximately 10-20% rate of biochemically relevant fistula), delayed gastric emptying, bile leak, wound infection, intra-abdominal abscess, hemorrhage, venous thromboembolism, pneumonia, and general anesthetic risks — as well as longer-term consequences including pancreatic exocrine insufficiency requiring enzyme replacement and high likelihood of post-operative endocrine insufficiency (new or worsening diabetes). We discussed that given her pre-existing diabetes and the degree of neoadjuvant treatment, including prior chemoradiation (which affects tissue healing), her risk for pancreatic fistula and wound complications may be modestly elevated above baseline. We also discussed that intraoperative findings might reveal disease that precludes resection (approximately 10-15% of planned pancreatectomies are aborted, though this is lower in patients who have had extensive neoadjuvant therapy and favorable pre-operative imaging). She verbalized understanding and signed written informed consent. She was given the opportunity to ask questions; she and her daughter asked thoughtful questions about recovery time and return to activity, which were answered in detail.

5. Surgeon's assessment of resectability: Based on clinical, laboratory, and radiographic assessment, I believe Ms. Osei-Bonsu is technically resectable with a high likelihood of achieving an R0 resection. The response she has demonstrated — both radiographically and with respect to tumor markers — is one of the better responses we see in the neoadjuvant pancreatic cancer setting. The decline in CA 19-9 by over 94% and the clear shrinkage and regression of vascular contact speak to meaningful treatment effect. I am optimistic about this case but have counseled the patient that pathologic findings at the time of resection will ultimately determine the next steps in her care.

6. Scheduling: Surgery has been scheduled. Pre-operative blood work (CBC, CMP, coagulation, type and screen, HbA1c, repeat CA 19-9) to be drawn on the morning of pre-anesthesia evaluation. Patient will be admitted the morning of surgery (same-day admission).

7. Follow-up: Patient will be seen by the hepatobiliary surgical team in clinic post-operatively. Medical oncology has been notified of planned surgical date and will reassess for adjuvant therapy planning after adequate post-operative recovery, typically 8-12 weeks post-operatively, contingent on final pathology.

Signed: [Attending Hepatobiliary Surgical Oncologist]
Co-signed: [Surgical Oncology Fellow]

---
Document dictated and reviewed by attending surgeon.
